My distributor is taking 30% — is that normal and can you help me switch? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

30% is on the high end — industry standard is 15–30% for most aggregators (DistroKid: flat fee, TuneCore: 0–15%, CD Baby: 9%). At your revenue level, the cost difference matters. Switching distributors is operational — I'll flag Agent Zero to loop in our Distribution Chief for the migration logistics. Financially, here's what the savings look like: [calculation based on your monthly revenue].

I produce my own beats. Can I sell production stuff on the side — sample packs, beats, presets? Where and how much can I make? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Absolutely — production assets are one of the best passive/semi-passive revenue streams for producer-artists. Here's the full landscape:

Beat Sales: BeatStars (the industry standard): Setup: free tier (limited uploads) or Pro ($19.99/month for unlimited). Revenue: you keep 100% on the free tier, BeatStars takes 0% of direct sales (they monetize through subscriptions). Pricing: MP3 lease: $20-$50. WAV lease: $50-$100. Tracked-out stems: $100-$200. Exclusive rights: $300-$5,000+. Top producers on BeatStars earn $5,000-$50,000+/month. Realistic for a growing producer: $500-$2,000/month within the first year of consistent posting. Airbit: similar model, slightly smaller marketplace. You can also sell directly via your own website (Gumroad, Shopify) to avoid any platform fees.

Sample Packs: Splice: the dominant marketplace. Submit your sample pack to their Creator program. Royalty model: you earn per download (subscribers use credits, you get paid per credit used). Well-performing packs earn $2,000-$20,000+. Splice is selective — you need quality and a following. Bandcamp/Gumroad: sell packs directly. $10-$30 per pack. You keep 85-97%. Payhip: another direct sales option. Zero monthly fee, 5% transaction fee.

Presets & Templates: Sell synthesizer presets (Serum, Vital, Omnisphere), DAW templates, or mixing chains. Platforms: Gumroad, your own site, or preset-specific marketplaces like PluginBoutique. Pricing: $10-$50 per preset pack, $15-$75 per DAW template.

Revenue math: 10 beat leases/month at $50 average = $500. 1 exclusive sale/month at $500 = $500. 1 sample pack earning $200/month on Splice = $200. Preset packs + templates: $100/month. Total: $1,300/month or $15,600/year — and this SCALES with your catalog size.

Key tips: Post new beats consistently (2-3 per week on BeatStars, with YouTube/TikTok previews). Use type beats in titles for discoverability: '[Artist Name] Type Beat.' Create a content flywheel: the TikTok preview drives BeatStars traffic. Keep master rights clarity: your beat leases should have clear terms about what the buyer can and cannot do.

I want to start selling directly to fans instead of relying on streaming. What platforms should I use — Bandcamp, Patreon, Fourthwall, Gumroad? How do I build this revenue stream? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Direct-to-fan (D2F) is the highest-margin revenue stream available to indie artists. You keep 80-95% of every dollar vs 30-40% through traditional channels. Here's the 2026 platform landscape:

One-Time Sales Platforms:

Bandcamp: 82% to artist (Bandcamp takes 15% digital, 10% physical + payment processing). Best for: album/single sales, vinyl, cassettes, merch. Bandcamp Fridays (fee waiver days) drive massive sales spikes. The audience expects to pay premium prices and tips generously. Recommendation: your primary storefront for music sales.

Gumroad: 10% flat fee + payment processing. Best for: digital products — sample packs, stems, tutorials, presets, production templates. Clean checkout. Less music-native than Bandcamp but great for supplementary products.

Recurring/Subscription Platforms:

Patreon: 5-12% fee (depends on tier). Best for: ongoing relationship with superfans. Tiers at $3, $5, $10, $25/month offering early access, behind-the-scenes, exclusive tracks, monthly Q&As. 200 patrons at $7 average = $1,400/month = $16,800/year. Proven model for musicians.

Fourthwall: 0% platform fee on memberships (only payment processing ~3%). Best for: merch + membership combo. Built for creators. Integrates storefronts with membership tiers. Growing fast among music creators in 2026.

Ko-fi: 0% fee on donations (5% on shop sales). Best for: low-commitment fan support. 'Buy me a coffee' model. Good entry point but lower revenue ceiling.

Building D2F revenue: (1) Start with Bandcamp for album sales + one subscription platform (Patreon or Fourthwall). (2) Create exclusive value: unreleased demos, acoustic versions, production breakdowns, early access, direct interaction. (3) Promote at every touchpoint: email signature, Instagram bio, end of every YouTube video, at live shows. (4) Set a goal: convert 2-3% of your streaming audience to direct supporters. At 50,000 monthly listeners, that's 1,000-1,500 potential customers.

The math: 500 fans buying one album/year at $10 on Bandcamp = $4,250 net. 100 Patreon subscribers at $7/month = $7,560 net/year. Combined: $11,810 from D2F alone — often more than streaming for artists under 500k monthly listeners.

What's a typical streaming rate on Apple Music vs Spotify? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance"}]

Looking that up in our knowledge base... Apple Music pays approximately $0.007–$0.01/stream (higher because they don't offer free tiers). Spotify averages $0.003–$0.005/stream. Tidal pays $0.0125/stream (highest major DSP). Amazon Music: $0.004/stream. These are averages — actual payouts vary by listener country, subscription tier, and quarterly fluctuations.

My distributor's royalty statement shows way less than what Spotify for Artists says I earned. How do I figure out where my money is going? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

This is one of the most common frustrations for indie artists and there are usually rational explanations — but you should absolutely investigate. Here's a systematic approach:

Why numbers differ: (1) Timing lag — Spotify for Artists shows estimated real-time data. Your distributor receives actual payments from Spotify 2-3 months later, then processes for another 2-4 weeks. A stream in January may not show on your distributor statement until April. (2) Currency conversion — Spotify pays in multiple currencies. Your distributor converts to USD at the time of processing, which may differ from the rate when streams occurred. (3) Spotify for Artists shows ALL streams, but not all generate revenue — streams under the 1,000/year threshold per track don't earn royalties. Free-tier streams pay significantly less than Premium streams. (4) Distributor fees — if you're on CD Baby (9%) or AWAL (15%), their cut is deducted before your statement.

How to audit: (1) Export your Spotify for Artists data (monthly stream counts per track). (2) Export your distributor's royalty report (usually CSV download). (3) Compare track-by-track for the SAME reporting period (not the same calendar month — adjust for the 2-3 month lag). (4) Calculate your effective per-stream rate: total payment / total streams. If it's below $0.003, something may be off. If it's $0.003-$0.005, that's normal range for 2026.

Red flags that warrant escalation: Per-stream rate below $0.002 consistently. Missing tracks (streams showing in Spotify for Artists but absent from distributor report). Sudden unexplained drops in revenue without corresponding stream drops. Streams from certain territories not appearing at all.

What to do: Contact your distributor's support with specific data: 'Track X had Y streams in Month Z per Spotify for Artists, but your report shows only W streams.' Most discrepancies resolve to timing. If they can't explain, consider switching distributors — trust is the foundation of that relationship.

I got a sync placement in a Netflix show. What should I expect to earn? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Netflix sync placement — breakdown: Upfront Sync Fee: typically $5,000–$50,000 for a major show (depends on scene length, exclusivity, territory). Split: 50% master recording fee (to you/label), 50% publishing fee (to publisher/PRO). Backend: performance royalties via your PRO (ASCAP/BMI) as the show airs globally — can be $1,000–$10,000+ annually for a hit show. Total 3-year value: $15k–$70k+ depending on negotiation. Did you negotiate a backend royalty clause? That's where the long-term value lives.

A clothing brand wants to sponsor my next music video for $3,000. They want me to wear their stuff and post 3 times on socials. Is this a good deal? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true,"artistTier":"emerging-indie"}]

Let's evaluate this deal by the numbers and the fine print:

Is $3,000 fair? It depends on your audience size and engagement. The industry standard for influencer/creator rates in 2026: Nano (1k-10k followers): $100-$500 per post. Micro (10k-50k): $500-$2,000 per post. Mid-tier (50k-250k): $2,000-$10,000 per post. So 3 social posts plus music video integration for $3,000 total is reasonable if you're in the 10k-50k range. If you're above 50k engaged followers, you're undercharging.

Key questions to ask before accepting: (1) Usage rights — Can they use your content in THEIR ads? For how long? This is worth $2,000-$10,000+ extra if they want paid media rights. (2) Exclusivity — Are you locked out of competing brand deals? For how long? Exclusivity should cost them 2-3x the base rate. (3) Deliverables — What exactly counts as a 'post'? Story vs feed vs Reel matters. How long must posts stay up? (4) Approval process — Do you get final creative approval on the music video integration?

Negotiation tips: Counter at $4,500-$5,000. Add a performance bonus (e.g., +$500 if posts exceed 100k combined impressions). Limit usage rights to their social channels only, not paid ads. Cap exclusivity at 30 days.

Long-term value: Brand deals compound. Your first deal at $3k leads to case studies that help you land $10k deals next time. Document everything — screenshots, analytics, engagement metrics — for your media kit.

I want to start a Patreon or some kind of paid subscription for my fans. What should I offer at each tier and what can I realistically make? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Direct-to-fan memberships are one of the most stable, predictable revenue streams an indie artist can build. Here's the proven tier structure:

Platform options: Patreon (industry standard, takes 5-12% depending on plan). Fourthwall (integrated with merch — great for musicians). Ko-fi (simpler, 0% platform fee on memberships). Bandzoogle (musician-specific, built-in website + subscriptions). Buy Me a Coffee (casual, lower commitment). Apple Music for Artists Fan Clubs (new in 2026).

Proven tier structure: Tier 1 — 'Supporter' ($3-$5/month): Early access to new music (24-48 hours before public release). Behind-the-scenes content (studio clips, voice memos). Community access (private Discord or community feed). Your name in credits. This is your volume tier — most subscribers will be here.

Tier 2 — 'Inner Circle' ($10-$15/month): Everything in Tier 1 plus: monthly demos/unreleased tracks. Monthly live stream/Q&A (exclusive). Discount code for merch (10-15%). Voting rights on creative decisions (cover art options, setlist input).

Tier 3 — 'VIP' ($25-$50/month): Everything in Tier 2 plus: handwritten lyric sheet or signed items quarterly. Personal video message on birthdays. Early/priority access to show tickets. Name on liner notes/credits.

Tier 4 — 'Executive Producer' ($100+/month — limit to 10-20 slots): Everything in Tier 3 plus: monthly 1-on-1 Zoom call. Credit on releases. Input on creative process. Exclusive merch not available anywhere else.

Revenue math: 100 Supporters at $5 = $500/month. 30 Inner Circle at $12 = $360/month. 10 VIPs at $30 = $300/month. 3 EPs at $100 = $300/month. Total: $1,460/month ($17,520/year). Patreon cut (8%): -$117/month. Net: $1,343/month ($16,116/year).

You need approximately 1,000-5,000 Spotify monthly listeners to realistically convert 100+ patrons. The conversion rate from listener to patron is typically 1-3%.

Retention is everything: The average Patreon subscriber stays for 4-8 months. To maximize retention: deliver consistently (never skip a month). Make members FEEL special — address them by name, respond to comments. Use polls and input to make them feel ownership in your art. The community aspect (Discord, community feed) often matters more than the content itself.

A distributor is offering me $50,000 as an advance against my royalties. They want a 3-year exclusive deal with 25% commission. I currently make about $4,000/month from streaming. Should I take it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"established","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

Let me model this with `model_revenue_waterfall` because the math tells the whole story.

**The deal structure:**
- Advance: $50,000 (paid to you upfront)
- Commission: 25% of all royalties
- Term: 3 years exclusive
- Your current revenue: ~$4,000/month = $48,000/year

**The math over 3 years:**
- Gross revenue (at current rate): $48,000 × 3 = $144,000
- Their 25% commission: $36,000
- Your net without advance: $108,000 over 3 years
- With $50,000 advance that recoups from your share: they take your royalties until the advance is recouped, THEN you get paid at the 75% rate

**Recoupment timeline:**
- Your monthly royalties to recoup: $4,000 × 75% = $3,000/month going to recoup the advance
- Months to recoup $50k: ~17 months
- After recoup: you earn $3,000/month for the remaining 19 months = $57,000
- Total you receive over 3 years: $50,000 (advance) + $57,000 (post-recoup) = $107,000

**Without this deal (staying DIY):**
- Total over 3 years at 0% commission (or DistroKid's flat fee): ~$144,000 - ~$60/year = $143,820

**The difference: You're giving up approximately $37,000 over 3 years for $50,000 in your pocket today.**

**When this deal makes sense:**
- You need capital NOW for a specific investment that will grow your career (album production, tour van, marketing campaign) and can't access it any other way
- You're confident your growth trajectory will outpace the commission loss

**When this deal doesn't make sense:**
- If you'll spend the $50k on living expenses — you're essentially borrowing from your future self at a high cost
- If you could get a traditional small business loan at 8-10% interest, that would cost you far less than the $37,000 this deal costs

**Negotiate these terms before walking away:**
1. Lower commission: 15-18% instead of 25%
2. Shorter term: 2 years instead of 3
3. Non-exclusive: keep the right to switch distributors
4. Revenue cap: once they've earned back 1.5x the advance, the commission drops to 10%

I've heard SoundExchange holds millions in unclaimed royalties for artists. I've never checked if I have any. Can you look this up for me? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Opening SoundExchange to check for unclaimed royalties. Running `browser_tool` to navigate to SoundExchange's royalty lookup.

SoundExchange context: SoundExchange collects and distributes digital performance royalties for sound recordings (the master, not the composition) from services like Pandora, SiriusXM, iHeart, and non-interactive streaming. This is SEPARATE from your PRO royalties. As a performing artist who owns your masters, you're entitled to these funds.

They currently hold over $1 billion in unmatched royalties across their system — many indie artists simply don't know to claim them.

To check your status: if you're already registered, I'll retrieve your SoundExchange credentials and pull up your dashboard. If not registered yet: I can walk you through SoundExchange registration directly. The process takes about 15 minutes, and registration is free. You'll need: your legal name, stage name(s), citizenship status, and a list of your sound recordings.

Can you securely save my ASCAP login credentials so I don't have to keep entering them every time I check my royalty statements? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true}]

Storing your ASCAP credentials in the secure vault now. Running `credential_vault` with action: store.

Your credentials are encrypted at rest using AES-256 and stored in the platform's secure credential store — never in plain text, never accessible to anyone other than you.

Once stored, I can use `credential_vault` with action: retrieve to access your ASCAP account automatically when checking royalty statements, pulling payment history, or auditing your registered catalog — no manual login needed.

Note: I'll never display your credentials back to you for security. If you need to update them (e.g., if your password changes), just let me know and I'll overwrite the stored entry.

I'm pulling about 2 million streams a month and I'm fully independent. A label keeps reaching out. Do I actually need them? [Context: {"currentModule":"finance","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing"}}]

At 2M streams/month, let's run the numbers before you make any decisions.

2M streams/month = ~24M streams/year. At a blended $0.003-$0.004 per stream (varies by DSP mix), that's $72,000-$96,000/year in streaming royalties alone — flowing directly to you through your distributor at roughly 80-85% of the gross (after distributor fees).

Now model what a typical indie label deal does to that:
- Label offers you a $150k advance. Sounds good.
- At a generous 70/30 split (you keep 70%), your per-stream earn drops from ~$0.0032 to ~$0.00224.
- You need ~67M streams just to recoup the advance at that rate — about 3 years at your current velocity.
- During recoupment: you see $0 from streaming royalties. After recoupment: you're earning ~$54k/year instead of the $77k you'd earn independently.

The math usually doesn't favor a deal at your level — UNLESS the label is actually bringing something you can't replicate: specific sync connections, a marketing infrastructure that will meaningfully grow your numbers, radio relationships, or international distribution you genuinely can't access.

The right question to ask before any meeting: 'What will you do in the next 12 months that I can't do myself, and how will you measure that?' If they can't answer it specifically, you have your answer.

Your leverage is highest right now — before you sign anything. Use it to negotiate better terms, not to rush into a deal.
